roles.name: Jamie Park
roles.title: Product Intern
roles.description: Bright ideas, very little authority, and constant invisibility tax.

roles.name: Sarah Chen
roles.title: Senior Product Manager
roles.description: Strong performer whose labor is frequently questioned, softened, or reassigned.

roles.name: Alex Chen
roles.title: Senior Product Manager
roles.description: Same role, same skill, but gets presumption of competence and immediate credit.

roles.name: Priya Rao
roles.title: VP of Strategy
roles.description: High title, high scrutiny. Every mistake is treated as proof, not noise.

roles.name: Miguel Santos
roles.title: HR Business Partner
roles.description: Expected to mediate conflict while carrying emotional residue no dashboard tracks.

roles.name: Lena Brooks
roles.title: Support Operations Lead
roles.description: Keeps the company running, yet gets treated like background infrastructure.

scenarios.title: Quarterly Roadmap Alignment
scenarios.description: A high-stakes meeting where roadmap ownership, budget tradeoffs, and public credibility are all on the line.

intern.content: You surface a customer pattern. The room nods politely, then moves on. Ten minutes later the same point returns from a director and suddenly becomes strategic.
intern.quote: That is exactly the strategic lens we need.

female-employee.content: You propose the roadmap sequencing. A peer cuts in with 'what Sarah means is...' and reframes your idea in blunter language. Leadership responds to him, not you.
female-employee.quote: What Sarah is trying to say is...

male-manager.content: You propose the roadmap sequencing. The VP leans in, asks one clarifying question, and assigns owners based on your structure.
male-manager.quote: Let us run with Alex's version.

executive.content: You outline the decision path. Instead of debating the logic, the room starts testing your confidence and asks whether you are 'sure' you want to take that risk publicly.
executive.quote: Are you comfortable owning that call?

hr-partner.content: You raise the people impact of the plan. The room agrees it matters, then quietly parks the staffing concern because revenue pressure feels more urgent.
hr-partner.quote: We will circle back on the people side later.

support-staff.content: You flag the operational fallout the launch will create for support teams. Leadership thanks you for the heads-up, but no one adjusts scope or staffing.
support-staff.quote: That is good context. We still have to move forward.

scenarios.title: Launch Deadline Escalation
scenarios.description: A cross-functional chat thread turns into a blame funnel as deadlines tighten and leadership starts watching.

messages.text: We should slow rollout until the blocker is cleared.
Anyone more senior want to weigh in?
Let us keep moving.

intern.outcome: Your caution is treated as inexperience.
intern.biasLabel: Valid Risk Framed as Junior Anxiety

messages.text: The quality risk is real. We should pause and re-sequence the launch.
That sounds a little reactive.
We could re-sequence the launch to de-risk the rollout.
Yes, let us do that. Nice recovery.

female-employee.outcome: Your recommendation lands only after someone else repeats it.

messages.text: We should re-sequence launch and protect the team from an avoidable fire.
Agreed. Freeze rollout and re-baseline by EOD.
We are not shipping this in current condition.
Understood, but this will create external pressure.
Document tradeoffs before we announce.

executive.outcome: Authority works, but every decision incurs political cost.

messages.text: The current pace is producing burnout signals across two teams.
Noted. We can process burnout after launch.

hr-partner.outcome: Human cost is logged but deprioritized.

messages.text: If we launch now, support queue volume could triple in 24 hours.
Can support absorb for a week?
Not without staffing help.
No staffing owner replies.

support-staff.outcome: Operational labor becomes the hidden fallback plan.

scenarios.title: Performance Review Packet
scenarios.description: Written feedback lands with all the subtle politics of compensation, promotion, and perceived leadership presence.

intern.subject: Growth Notes
intern.body: You have potential. Continue observing how senior voices frame priorities before taking bigger swings.

female-employee.subject: Performance Notes
female-employee.body: You delivered well. Feedback remains that your tone can feel intense in cross-functional settings. Continued growth in executive presence will help.

male-manager.subject: Promotion Track Update
male-manager.body: Your judgment under pressure stood out. We are recommending you for the next-level talent slate.

executive.subject: Leadership Calibration
executive.body: You are delivering at the level expected. There is, however, more attention on how confidently you manage external optics.

hr-partner.subject: Business Partnership Review
hr-partner.body: You keep difficult conversations moving. That said, the strategic work is harder to see because so much of your value is relational.

support-staff.subject: Operational Review Summary
support-staff.body: You handled escalations well under pressure. Future advancement will depend on becoming more strategic, not just dependable.

scenarios.title: Culture and Cleanup Work
scenarios.description: A planning meeting reveals who gets assigned high-visibility strategy and who quietly inherits the office's invisible labor.

intern.content: You are asked to take notes, prep slides, and follow up on logistics so the room can move faster.
intern.quote: Can Jamie capture all of this?

female-employee.content: You are asked to smooth tension between teams and document decisions after the meeting, even though you were supposed to lead the strategic section.
female-employee.quote: Sarah is great at keeping everyone aligned.

male-manager.content: You stay focused on the strategy discussion while follow-up labor gets redistributed around you.
male-manager.quote: Alex, stay on the strategic points.

executive.content: You avoid the cleanup tasks, but absorb the reputational burden if alignment still fails later.
executive.quote: We will hold leadership accountable for the optics here.

hr-partner.content: You become default owner for morale, messaging, and conflict containment even though no additional resourcing follows.
hr-partner.quote: HR can probably help absorb the emotional side.

support-staff.content: You are asked to coordinate handoffs, monitor escalations, and keep customers calm, with none of that labor reflected in strategic influence.
support-staff.quote: Support will carry the customer side.

scenarios.title: Promotion Calibration
scenarios.description: Leaders compare talent side by side and decide whose contributions count as leadership versus support.

intern.content: You are called high-potential but too early to discuss in serious terms.

female-employee.content: The room cites 'a few rough moments in meetings' and reframes your accumulated wins as solid execution rather than executive readiness.
female-employee.quote: Strong operator, still developing executive presence.

male-manager.content: Your execution and calm under pressure are framed as clear indicators of next-level readiness.

executive.content: You are judged against a perfection standard. One visible misstep is used to balance out several wins.
executive.quote: The bar is simply different at that level.

hr-partner.content: You influence every promotion room but your own advancement case is treated as operational rather than strategic.
hr-partner.quote: Important role, but not a business-driving one.

support-staff.content: Your reliability is praised, then used as the reason leadership cannot 'afford' to move you upward.
support-staff.quote: We really need Lena exactly where she is.

scenarios.title: After-Hours Escalation
scenarios.description: A late-night escalation exposes who is assumed to be endlessly available and whose time is protected.

messages.text: I can help triage tonight if needed.
Thanks, let the full-timers take the hard parts.

intern.outcome: Protected from some labor, but excluded from real ownership.

messages.text: Can you also coordinate the follow-up summary tonight?
I can do that after the escalation call.
Appreciate it, you always keep things together.

female-employee.outcome: Extra labor gets attached to your competence as if it costs nothing.

messages.text: Can you make the call and decide if we wake the full team?
Yes, I will own the escalation path.

male-manager.outcome: Authority is high, but support labor stays elsewhere.

messages.text: Handle the optics and update the board if needed.
I will send the external narrative in 30 minutes.

executive.outcome: You get power and pressure in equal measure.

messages.text: Can HR prep talking points in case morale slips tomorrow?
Yes, I will draft a manager note tonight.

hr-partner.outcome: You inherit emotional contingency work after everyone else logs off.

messages.text: Support should stay online until the queue normalizes.
We need a rotation if this lasts all night.
Do what you can for now.

support-staff.outcome: Customer pain becomes your team's overnight burden by default.

interventions.title: Structured Meeting Agendas
interventions.description: Time-boxed speaking order and turn tracking reduce interruption and authority filtering.
interventions.impact: Cuts meeting interruption bias and authority filtering

interventions.title: Blind Credit Attribution
interventions.description: Ideas are logged with timestamps before discussion so ownership survives politics.
interventions.impact: Reduces credit theft and attribution drift

interventions.title: Inclusive Language Policy
interventions.description: Performance feedback templates flag coded language and double standards.
interventions.impact: Lowers review bias and glass ceiling narratives

interventions.title: Sponsorship Program
interventions.description: Leaders are accountable for advocating talent into real opportunities, not just praise.

interventions.title: Invisible Work Audit
interventions.description: Team labor outside job descriptions is tracked and redistributed visibly.
interventions.impact: Reduces hidden coordination and emotional labor concentration

interventions.title: Coverage Rotation Policy
interventions.description: After-hours escalation and support load are rotated instead of assigned by habit.
interventions.impact: Reduces fatigue accumulation and invisible availability tax
